epigraph.text:
The purposes of a person's heart are deep waters, but 
one who has insight draws them out.

content.text:
If a function is a story, its derivative is the narrator's commentary: it 
tells us, at every moment, whether the story is rising or falling, 
accelerating or slowing, reaching a peak or plunging to a valley. In 
Section [sec:deriv-as-function] we learned to compute $f'$ as a 
function and to graph it alongside $f$. Now we develop the language to 
describe exactly what $f'$ reveals about $f$.

This section is a *preview*. We will establish the key vocabulary—increasing, 
decreasing, critical point, concavity, inflection point—and illustrate each 
idea with functions whose derivatives we can compute from the limit definition. 
In Chapter 5, once we have the differentiation rules of Chapter 4, we will 
develop these ideas into a complete theory.
Position, Velocity, and Acceleration

The most vivid interpretation of derivatives comes from motion along a 
straight line. If an object's position at time $t$ is given by 
$s(t)$, then:

items:
The **velocity** is $v(t) = s'(t)$—the rate of change of 
 position.

The **acceleration** is $a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t)$—the rate of 
 change of velocity.

The **speed** is $\abs{v(t)}$—velocity without regard to 
 direction.

content.text:
Velocity tells us *how fast* and *in which direction* the object 
moves. Acceleration tells us how the velocity itself is changing: is the 
object speeding up, slowing down, or maintaining a constant speed?

content.title: Position, velocity, and acceleration
content.problem:
A ball is thrown upward with initial velocity $64$ ft/s from a height of 
$80$ feet. Its position is $s(t) = 80 + 64t - 16t^{2}$. Analyze its 
motion.
content.solution:
**Velocity.** We compute $v(t) = s'(t)$ from the definition:
$$
 s'(t) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{[80 + 64(t+h) - 16(t+h)^{2}] - [80 + 64t - 16t^{2}]}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{64h - 16(2th + h^{2})}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{64h - 32th - 16h^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (64 - 32t - 16h) \\[6pt]
 &= 64 - 32t.
$$
So $v(t) = 64 - 32t$.

*Acceleration.** We differentiate $v(t)$:
$$
 a(t) = v'(t) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{[64 - 32(t+h)] - [64 - 32t]}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{-32h}{h} = -32.
$$
The acceleration is a constant $-32$ ft/s$^{2}$ (gravity).

*Analysis.**

- At $t = 0$: $v(0) = 64$ ft/s (moving upward).

- The ball reaches its peak when $v(t) = 0$: 
 $64 - 32t = 0 \implies t = 2$ seconds.

- At $t = 2$: $s(2) = 80 + 128 - 64 = 144$ feet (maximum height).

- For $t > 2$: $v(t) < 0$ (the ball is falling).

- The constant negative acceleration means the velocity decreases at 
 a steady rate of $32$ ft/s every second.

content.text: Increasing and Decreasing Functions

In Section [sec:deriv-as-function] we observed a simple but powerful 
pattern: where $f$ rises, $f'$ is positive; where $f$ falls, $f'$ is 
negative. Let us now make this precise.

content.title: Increasing and Decreasing Functions
content.content:
A function $f$ is **increasing** on an 
interval $I$ if, for every pair $x_{1}, x_{2}$ in $I$ with $x_{1} < x_{2}$, 
we have $f(x_{1}) < f(x_{2})$.

A function $f$ is **decreasing** on an 
interval $I$ if, for every pair $x_{1}, x_{2}$ in $I$ with $x_{1} < x_{2}$, 
we have $f(x_{1}) > f(x_{2})$.

content.text:
The connection to the derivative is intuitively clear and will be proved 
rigorously in Chapter 5 (using the Mean Value Theorem):

content.title: The Increasing/Decreasing Test (Preview)
content.content:
Let $f$ be continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$.

- If $f'(x) > 0$ for all $x$ in $(a,b)$, then $f$ is increasing 
 on $[a,b]$.

- If $f'(x) < 0$ for all $x$ in $(a,b)$, then $f$ is decreasing 
 on $[a,b]$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*We will prove this theorem in Chapter 5 once we have the 
Mean Value Theorem. For now, accept it as geometrically obvious: a function 
with positive slope everywhere must be rising.*

content.title: Finding where $f$ increases and decreases
content.problem:
Let $f(x) = x^{2}$. We know $f'(x) = 2x$.

- $f'(x) > 0$ when $x > 0$, so $f$ is increasing on $(0, \infty)$.

- $f'(x) < 0$ when $x < 0$, so $f$ is decreasing on $(-\infty, 0)$.

- $f'(0) = 0$: the tangent is horizontal at the vertex.
This matches the familiar parabola opening upward.

content.title: Increasing and decreasing intervals for $x^{2} + 3x$
content.problem:
Let $f(x) = x^{2} + 3x$. We showed in Example [ex:deriv-fn-x2plus3x] 
that $f'(x) = 2x + 3$.

- $f'(x) = 0$ when $x = -\dfrac{3}{2}$.

- $f'(x) > 0$ when $x > -\dfrac{3}{2}$: $f$ is increasing.

- $f'(x) < 0$ when $x < -\dfrac{3}{2}$: $f$ is decreasing.
The function has a minimum at $x = -\dfrac{3}{2}$.

content.text: Critical Points and Local Extrema

content.title: Critical Point
content.content:
A number $c$ in the domain of $f$ is a **critical point** 
(or **critical number**) of $f$ if either $f'(c) = 0$ or $f'(c)$ 
does not exist.

content.text:
Critical points are the "candidates" for local maxima and minima. At a 
local maximum, the function stops increasing and begins decreasing—so the 
derivative is zero (assuming it exists). At a corner or cusp, the 
derivative fails to exist, but the function can still have a peak or valley.

content.title: Local Maximum and Minimum
content.content:
A function $f$ has a **local maximum** at $c$ if 
$f(c) \ge f(x)$ for all $x$ near $c$ (in some open interval containing $c$).

A function $f$ has a **local minimum** at $c$ if 
$f(c) \le f(x)$ for all $x$ near $c$.

content.title: Fermat's Theorem (Preview)
content.content:
If $f$ has a local maximum or minimum at $c$, and if $f'(c)$ exists, 
then $f'(c) = 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*The proof will appear in Chapter 5. The intuition is 
straightforward: at a peak, the tangent line is horizontal.*

content.title: The converse of Fermat's Theorem is false
content.content:
A point where $f'(c) = 0$ is not necessarily a local extremum. For 
example, $f(x) = x^{3}$ has $f'(0) = 0$, but $x = 0$ is neither a 
local max nor a local min—the function passes right through, continuing 
to increase.

content.title: Identifying critical points
content.problem:
Find the critical points of each function:

1. $f(x) = x^{2} - 4x$

2. $g(x) = x^{3}$

3. $h(x) = \abs{x}$
content.solution:
1. We compute $f'(x)$ from the limit definition:
 $$
 f'(x) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{[(x+h)^{2} - 4(x+h)] - [x^{2} - 4x]}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{2xh + h^{2} - 4h}{h} = 2x - 4.
 $$
 Setting $f'(x) = 0$: $2x - 4 = 0 \implies x = 2$. The only critical 
 point is $x = 2$. Since $f'$ changes from negative to positive at 
 $x = 2$, this is a local minimum.

2. We know $g'(x) = 3x^{2}$. Setting $g'(x) = 0$: 
 $3x^{2} = 0 \implies x = 0$. The critical point is $x = 0$. But 
 $g'(x) = 3x^{2} \ge 0$ on both sides of $0$, so $g$ does not change 
 direction—$x = 0$ is *not* a local extremum.

3. We know $h'(x) = 1$ for $x > 0$ and $h'(x) = -1$ for $x < 0$, 
 and $h'(0)$ does not exist. So $x = 0$ is a critical point (because 
 the derivative does not exist there). Since $h'$ changes from $-1$ 
 to $+1$, $x = 0$ is a local minimum.

content.text: Concavity and the Second Derivative

The first derivative tells us whether $f$ is increasing or decreasing. The 
second derivative tells us *how* $f$ is increasing or decreasing—is 
the curve bending upward or downward?

content.title: Concavity
content.content:
A function $f$ is **concave up** on an 
interval $I$ if $f'$ is increasing on $I$. Geometrically, the curve 
"opens upward" and lies above its tangent lines.

A function $f$ is **concave down** on 
$I$ if $f'$ is decreasing on $I$. The curve "opens downward" and lies 
below its tangent lines.

content.text:
Since $f'$ is increasing precisely when its derivative $(f')' = f''$ is 
positive, we get the following test:

content.title: Concavity Test (Preview)
content.content:
Let $f$ be twice differentiable on an interval $I$.

- If $f''(x) > 0$ on $I$, then $f$ is concave up on $I$.

- If $f''(x) < 0$ on $I$, then $f$ is concave down on $I$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Concavity of $x^{2}$
content.problem:
For $f(x) = x^{2}$, we have $f'(x) = 2x$ and $f''(x) = 2$. Since 
$f''(x) = 2 > 0$ for all $x$, the parabola is concave up everywhere. 
This matches the familiar "U-shape" of the graph.

content.title: Concavity of $x^{3}$
content.problem:
For $f(x) = x^{3}$, we have $f'(x) = 3x^{2}$ and $f''(x) = 6x$.

- $f''(x) > 0$ when $x > 0$: concave up.

- $f''(x) < 0$ when $x < 0$: concave down.

- $f''(0) = 0$: the concavity changes at the origin.
The point $(0, 0)$ is where the curve transitions from bending downward 
(for $x < 0$) to bending upward (for $x > 0$).

content.text: Inflection Points

content.title: Inflection Point
content.content:
A point $(c, f(c))$ is an **inflection point** 
of $f$ if $f$ is continuous at $c$ and the concavity of $f$ changes at $c$ 
(from up to down, or from down to up).

content.text:
At an inflection point, the second derivative typically changes sign. If 
$f''$ exists at the inflection point, then $f''(c) = 0$. However, 
$f''(c) = 0$ alone does not guarantee an inflection point—the sign of 
$f''$ must actually *change*.

content.title: Inflection point of $x^{3}$
content.problem:
We showed that $f(x) = x^{3}$ has $f''(x) = 6x$. Since $f''$ changes 
sign at $x = 0$ (negative for $x < 0$, positive for $x > 0$), the point 
$(0, 0)$ is an inflection point.

content.title: No inflection despite $f''(c) = 0$
content.problem:
Consider $f(x) = x^{4}$. Using the limit definition (or assuming the 
pattern from our earlier computations): $f'(x) = 4x^{3}$ and 
$f''(x) = 12x^{2}$. We have $f''(0) = 0$, but $f''(x) = 12x^{2} \ge 0$ 
for all $x$—the sign does not change. So $(0, 0)$ is *not* an 
inflection point; the function is concave up on both sides of the origin.

content.text: Graphical Analysis: From $f$ to $f'$ and Back

A powerful skill is reading the graph of $f$ and deducing the graph of 
$f'$—and vice versa.

content.title: Sketching $f'$ from the Graph of $f$
content.content:
Given the graph of $f$:

1. Identify where $f$ has horizontal tangents ($f' = 0$): mark these 
 as $x$-intercepts of $f'$.

2. Identify where $f$ is increasing ($f' > 0$) and decreasing 
 ($f' < 0$): the graph of $f'$ is above/below the $x$-axis.

3. Estimate the steepness of $f$ to judge the magnitude of $f'$.

4. Identify where $f$ has corners or cusps: $f'$ has jumps or 
 vertical asymptotes at those points.

content.title: Sketching $f'$ from a graph of $f$
content.problem:
Consider the graph of $f$ shown below: a smooth curve with a local 
maximum at $x = 1$ and a local minimum at $x = 3$.

center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 axis lines=middle,
 xlabel={$x$},
 ylabel={$y$},
 xmin=-0.5, xmax=5,
 ymin=-3, ymax=5,
 width=10cm, height=6cm,
 samples=100,
 grid=both,
 grid style={AtlasBorder, very thin},
 tick style={AtlasSecondary},
 tick label style={font=, AtlasSecondary},
 xtick={1,2,3,4},
 title={ Graph of $f$},
 title style={at={(0.5,1.05)}},
 ]
 
 {(1/3)*x^3 - 2*x^2 + 3*x + 1};
 axis
tikzpicture
center
content.solution:
Reading the graph of $f$:

- $f$ increases on $(0, 1)$, so $f'(x) > 0$ there.

- $f$ has a horizontal tangent at $x = 1$, so $f'(1) = 0$.

- $f$ decreases on $(1, 3)$, so $f'(x) < 0$ there.

- $f$ has a horizontal tangent at $x = 3$, so $f'(3) = 0$.

- $f$ increases on $(3, \infty)$, so $f'(x) > 0$ there.

The graph of $f'$ crosses the $x$-axis at $x = 1$ and $x = 3$, is 
positive before $x = 1$ and after $x = 3$, and is negative between them:

center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 axis lines=middle,
 xlabel={$x$},
 ylabel={$y$},
 xmin=-0.5, xmax=5,
 ymin=-2, ymax=4,
 width=10cm, height=5cm,
 samples=100,
 grid=both,
 grid style={AtlasBorder, very thin},
 tick style={AtlasSecondary},
 tick label style={font=, AtlasSecondary},
 xtick={1,2,3,4},
 title={ Graph of $f'$},
 title style={at={(0.5,1.05)}},
 ]
 {x^2 - 4*x + 3};
 axis
tikzpicture
center

The derivative $f'$ is itself a parabola opening upward, which is 
consistent with the original function being a cubic.

content.title: Complete analysis of $s(t) = t^{3} - 6t^{2} + 9t$
content.problem:
A particle moves along a line with position 
$s(t) = t^{3} - 6t^{2} + 9t$ meters at time $t \ge 0$ seconds. 
Analyze the motion completely.
content.solution:
**Velocity.** Using the limit definition (or the results we've 
built):
$$
 v(t) = s'(t) &= \limtozero{h} 
 \frac{[(t+h)^{3} - 6(t+h)^{2} + 9(t+h)] - [t^{3} - 6t^{2} + 9t]}{h}.
$$
Expanding $(t+h)^{3} = t^{3} + 3t^{2}h + 3th^{2} + h^{3}$ and 
$(t+h)^{2} = t^{2} + 2th + h^{2}$:
align*
 s'(t) &= h 
 3t^{2h + 3th^{2} + h^{3} - 12th - 6h^{2} + 9h}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (3t^{2} + 3th + h^{2} - 12t - 6h + 9) \\[6pt]
 &= 3t^{2} - 12t + 9.
\end{align*}
Factor: $v(t) = 3(t^{2} - 4t + 3) = 3(t - 1)(t - 3)$.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Acceleration.}
\[
 a(t) = v'(t) = 6t - 12.
\]

*Critical points of $s$:** $v(t) = 0$ when 
$t = 1$ and $t = 3$.

*Increasing/decreasing analysis:**

| Interval | Sign of $v(t)$ | Direction | $s$ is … |
| $(0, 1)$ | $+$ | rightward | increasing |
| $(1, 3)$ | $-$ | leftward | decreasing |
| $(3, \infty)$ | $+$ | rightward | increasing |

*Extrema:**

- At $t = 1$: $s(1) = 1 - 6 + 9 = 4$ meters (local maximum of position).

- At $t = 3$: $s(3) = 27 - 54 + 27 = 0$ meters (local minimum of position).

*Concavity:** $a(t) = 6t - 12 = 0$ when $t = 2$.

- For $t < 2$: $a(t) < 0$, velocity is decreasing (decelerating).

- For $t > 2$: $a(t) > 0$, velocity is increasing (accelerating).

- $t = 2$ is an inflection point of the position graph, and the 
 moment when the particle's deceleration switches to acceleration.

The particle starts at the origin, moves right to position $4$ m at $t=1$, 
reverses direction, returns to the origin at $t=3$, then moves right 
indefinitely.

content.text: A Look Ahead

The ideas in this section—increasing/decreasing behavior, critical points, 
concavity, inflection points—form the backbone of what is called 
**curve sketching**, which we develop fully in 
Chapter 5. The reason we cannot go further now is simple: to analyze 
interesting functions (polynomials of degree four or higher, rational 
functions, trigonometric functions), we need faster ways to compute 
derivatives. Computing $f'$ from the limit definition every time is 
possible in principle but excruciatingly slow in practice. In the next 
section, and then in all of Chapter 4, we develop the rules that make 
differentiation efficient.

marginNotes.text:
But those who hope in the Lord will renew 
their strength. They will soar on wings like eagles; they will run and 
not grow weary, they will walk and not be faint.

The Lord makes firm the steps of the 
one who delights in him; though he may stumble, he will not fall, for 
the Lord upholds him with his hand.

Trust in the Lord with all your heart 
and lean not on your own understanding; in all your ways submit to him, 
and he will make your paths straight.

There is a time for everything, and 
a season for every activity under the heavens.

marginNotes.author: Pierre-Simon Laplace
marginNotes.text:
All the effects of Nature are only 
the mathematical consequences of a small number of immutable laws.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
To explain all nature is too difficult a task 
for any one man or even for any one age.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Mathematicians have tried in vain to this 
day to discover some order in the sequence of prime numbers, and we have 
reason to believe that it is a mystery into which the human mind will 
never penetrate.
